title: What the heck is an RPG, anyway? (Solo RPG Friday)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_BvUrcl5tls

Daniel (@dungeondive) steps back from reviews for a discussion piece: what actually *is* a role-playing game? He starts from childhood make-believe — pitting G.I. Joe against Transformers against Star Wars — where the arguments were always about whose rules won. RPGs, he argues, are the governing framework that turns the Calvinball chaos of make-believe into the controlled chaos of Blood Bowl. D&D laid that bedrock: persistent characters defined on paper, a referee to keep things fair, collaborative storytelling, and rules for social as well as martial conflict.

From there he traces the long feedback loop between tabletop and screen — Rogue, Wizardry, Bard's Tale and Ultima borrowing from D&D; JRPGs and Diablo pushing the grind; gamebooks opening a solo door; then LARP, Microscope and Fiasco rejecting stats entirely — until video games began influencing tabletop in return (he name-checks 4e as a video-game-flavoured experiment).

He closes with seven personal markers of an RPG, weighted toward solo play: looser structure with room to improvise, portable persistent characters and worlds, the freedom to bolt on outside random tools, the option to focus on just one pillar, permission to tailor everything to mood, an actively *experienced* story, and little or no concern with winning or losing.

----

What are *your* defining elements of an RPG — and which of Daniel's seven would you add to, cut, or argue with?
